points.text: Use wss (TLS) for all WebSocket connections to encrypt traffic and prevent man‑in‑the‑middle.
Authenticate each connection with a short‑lived signed token (e.g., JWT) and verify on upgrade.
Include a monotonically increasing sequence number in every event payload per client or stream.
Server tracks the highest sequence number processed per client; drop any event with lower or equal number.
Attach a unique event ID (UUID) and keep a recent ID cache (e.g., LRU) to deduplicate retransmissions.
Optionally embed a timestamp; if out‑of‑order, buffer until missing seq arrives or discard after timeout.
Use per‑message HMAC (or signed payload) to detect tampering and replay of old messages.
Rotate tokens on reconnect; invalidate previous tokens after a short grace period.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: The request about WebSocket ordering, deduplication, and token security is not within the SQL optimization domain.
UNKNOWN: No SQL schema, query, or transaction details were provided to give a database-specific answer.
Assign a monotonically increasing sequence number to each event per client session; discard any received with a lower number.
Attach a globally unique event ID (e.g., UUID) and keep a short‑term cache of recent IDs to drop duplicates.
Define a replay window (e.g., last N sequence numbers); ignore events outside this window to prevent replay attacks.
When a client reconnects, have it send the last sequence number it processed; resume sending from the next number.
Use WSS (TLS) to encrypt the channel; never place authentication tokens in query strings or plain‑text headers.
Issue short‑lived signed JWTs with a per‑connection nonce; server validates nonce and expiration on each message.
Rotate signing keys regularly and provide a revocation endpoint to invalidate compromised tokens immediately.
Implement WebSocket ping/pong keep‑alives and back‑pressure handling to detect dead connections quickly.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: The question concerns WebSocket ordering, deduplication, and security, which is outside the math_logic domain.
UNKNOWN: No answer can be provided within the specified domain scope.
Assign each message a server-generated monotonically increasing sequence number per client; drop lower numbers.
Include a unique message ID (UUID) and store recent IDs to reject duplicates.
Use a per‑connection nonce or timestamp signed with HMAC; reject messages with stale timestamps.
Require WebSocket upgrade over TLS (wss://) and verify Origin header to prevent cross‑site WS hijack.
Authenticate each connection with a short‑lived JWT containing a jti claim; reject reused jti values.
Rotate JWTs periodically and bind them to the client’s IP or device fingerprint.
Implement server‑side replay protection by maintaining a sliding window of accepted nonces/timestamps.
Use application‑level ACKs; client can request retransmission for missing sequence numbers.
